---

## Cofounder Design Review: Effective Pattern Normalization

### Market Fit

This is the right problem to solve. The asymmetry between friction (nicely clustered) and effective patterns (all 1x) is immediately visible to users and undermines the credibility of the Patterns page. If your "What Worked" section shows 8 items all at 1x, users reasonably conclude the tool cannot detect patterns. That is a product-breaking signal on the page whose entire job is pattern detection.

Obsidian's tag system and WakaTime's language breakdowns succeed because they show aggregated, recognizable categories rather than raw text. The category approach is sound.

### DX Assessment

**The hybrid display (category heading + description sub-items) is correct.** Category-only would strip the context that makes patterns actionable. Description-only is what we have today, and it does not work. The hybrid gives you the clustering for frequency credibility and the specifics for actionability.

**The 12-category taxonomy needs surgery.** Here are my concerns, organized by your review questions:

---

### 1. Category Taxonomy — Needs Pruning

**The good:** `structured-planning`, `verification-workflow`, `incremental-implementation`, `context-gathering`, `systematic-debugging`, and `self-correction` are all recognizable developer behaviors that span every tool. These 6 are strong.

**The problematic:**

| Category | Issue | Recommendation |
|----------|-------|----------------|
| `agent-delegation` | Only applies to Claude Code and Codex in agentic mode. Cursor/Copilot users will never see this. | MERGE into `structured-planning` or REMOVE. If you keep it, accept it will be a dead category for 3 of 5 source tools. |
| `type-driven-development` | Too niche. Most sessions will not be primarily about types-first design. This will be a perpetual 1x category. | MERGE into `domain-expertise`. Types-first is a subset of applying domain knowledge. |
| `test-driven-development` | Same problem. TDD is a methodology, not a pattern you observe per-session. An LLM seeing "wrote tests" will assign this; an LLM seeing "wrote tests first" is a subtle distinction it will fumble. | MERGE into `verification-workflow`. Writing tests first vs writing tests after is a workflow detail, not a separate category. |
| `documentation-practice` | Thin. How often does "wrote docs alongside code" surface as a top-3 effective pattern in a session? Rarely. | REMOVE. If it shows up, let it land in a catch-all or `verification-workflow`. |
| `version-control-discipline` | Clean commits and branching are hygiene, not a "technique that worked well." The LLM is unlikely to surface "made clean commits" as a top-3 pattern unless the session was specifically about git cleanup. | REMOVE or demote to a catch-all. |

**My proposed taxonomy: 8 categories, not 12.**

1. `structured-planning` — Task breakdown, phased plans, upfront design
2. `incremental-implementation` — Small steps, iterative building, progressive refinement
3. `verification-workflow` — Build/test/commit loops, validation before proceeding (absorbs TDD)
4. `systematic-debugging` — Binary search debugging, log diagnosis, isolation techniques
5. `self-correction` — Recognizing wrong path and pivoting
6. `context-gathering` — Reading code/docs before changes, understanding before acting
7. `domain-expertise` — Applying framework/library/language knowledge effectively (absorbs type-driven)
8. `effective-tooling` — Leveraging tool-specific capabilities well (absorbs agent-delegation for agentic tools, applies to Copilot completions, Cursor multi-file edits, etc.)

This gives you a taxonomy that works across all 5 source tools. `effective-tooling` is the key addition — it is the tool-neutral version of `agent-delegation` that captures "used the tool's capabilities well" regardless of whether the tool has agents.

**Why 8 and not 12:** Friction has 15 canonical categories but friction is inherently more varied (many things can go wrong). Effective patterns are more convergent (there are fewer ways things go right). 8 categories with healthy frequency counts are more credible than 12 categories where 4 are perpetually empty.

### 2. User Value — Yes, Clustering Helps

The current exact-match grouping produces a wall of unique descriptions at 1x each. This is noise, not insight. Users cannot see "I rely on planning before coding" when it is expressed 5 different ways across 5 sessions.

The key insight: **frequency is the signal.** "structured-planning: 14x" tells a developer "this is your strongest habit." A list of 8 unique descriptions at 1x tells them nothing.

The hybrid display is essential though. Under "structured-planning: 14x", showing the actual descriptions ("broke the feature into 3 phases", "created a task graph before coding") gives the developer concrete examples of what they actually did. This is the Spotify Wrapped pattern — category gives the headline, details give the story.

### 3. Naming Assessment

| Category | Intuitive? | Suggestion |
|----------|-----------|------------|
| `structured-planning` | Yes, immediately clear | Keep |
| `incremental-implementation` | Yes but verbose | Consider `iterative-building` — shorter, same meaning |
| `verification-workflow` | Slightly clinical | Consider `validate-before-proceeding` or keep as-is — developers know "verification" |
| `systematic-debugging` | Yes, strong | Keep |
| `self-correction` | Mostly clear, could be confused with LLM self-correction | Consider `course-correction` which already exists in your facet model |
| `context-gathering` | Yes | Keep |
| `domain-expertise` | Yes | Keep |
| `effective-tooling` | Clear enough | Keep |

One naming principle: these names will appear as headings on the dashboard. Run each through the test: "Would a developer screenshot this and share it?" If the name reads well as a badge label, it works.

### 4. Source Tool Breadth — The Critical Concern

This is my biggest flag. You support 5 source tools. Let me model what categories each tool's sessions are likely to populate:

| Category | Claude Code | Cursor | Codex CLI | Copilot CLI | Copilot Chat |
|----------|-------------|--------|-----------|-------------|--------------|
| `structured-planning` | High | Medium | High | Low | Low |
| `incremental-implementation` | High | High | High | Medium | Medium |
| `verification-workflow` | High | Medium | Medium | Low | Low |
| `systematic-debugging` | High | High | High | Medium | Medium |
| `self-correction` | High | Medium | High | Low | Low |
| `context-gathering` | High | High | Medium | Low | Medium |
| `domain-expertise` | High | High | High | High | High |
| `effective-tooling` | High | High | High | Medium | Medium |

A Copilot-only user might only populate 3-4 categories with any frequency. That is fine — showing 4 categories with real frequency is better than showing 8 at 1x. But you should ensure the UI gracefully handles sparse data. Do not show empty categories. This is where friction's bar chart works well — it only shows categories that have data.

### 5. Display Strategy

**The proposed hierarchy (category heading + description sub-items) is correct.** But I would push further:

- **Friction uses a bar chart. Effective patterns should NOT use a bar chart.** Friction is inherently negative — severity coloring and bar length communicate "how bad." Effective patterns are positive — they should feel different. A grouped list with frequency badges (what you have now, but clustered) is the right format. This visual contrast reinforces the "friction vs wins" framing.
- **Sort by frequency descending.** The top pattern should be the developer's strongest habit.
- **Cap displayed descriptions per category at 3.** Beyond 3 sub-items, the list becomes noise. Show "structured-planning: 14x" with 3 example descriptions and a "+11 more" indicator.
- **Confidence threshold.** You have `avg_confidence` on effective patterns. Consider hiding patterns below confidence 50. This is quality control — do not show a "win" the LLM was not confident about.

### 6. Growth Strategy

8 categories is a good ceiling for now. My expansion strategy:

- **Do not add categories proactively.** Wait for data. After 3 months, look at what the LLM assigns to categories and what falls through to "uncategorized."
- **Add a category only when uncategorized patterns cluster.** Same strategy that friction uses — the alias map in `friction-normalize.ts` emerged from observing real LLM outputs. Do the same for effective patterns.
- **Upper bound: 12-15 categories.** Beyond that, you are fighting the LLM's ability to consistently classify. Friction is at 15 canonical categories and already needs a normalizer with Levenshtein distance. Keep effective patterns simpler.

### Positioning Impact

This strengthens the product story. "Code Insights shows you your strongest coding habits across all your AI tools" is a much better pitch than "here are 8 unique descriptions at 1x." The Patterns page becomes genuinely insightful instead of being a data dump.

The cross-tool angle is the unique moat here. No competing tool (Claude's `/insights`, Cursor's usage data) can show you "structured-planning is your #1 pattern across Claude Code AND Cursor." That is the insight only Code Insights can deliver.

### Recommendation: PROCEED WITH CHANGES

The approach is right. The taxonomy needs pruning from 12 to 8 categories to avoid thin clusters and tool-specific dead categories. The hybrid display (category + descriptions) is the correct UX. Implementation should mirror the friction normalization pipeline — category in the LLM prompt, deterministic normalizer for variants, alias map that grows from observed data.

**Key changes before implementation:**
1. Reduce to 8 categories (see proposed list above)
2. Add `effective-tooling` as the tool-neutral replacement for `agent-delegation`
3. Merge TDD into `verification-workflow`, type-driven into `domain-expertise`
4. Drop `documentation-practice` and `version-control-discipline`
5. Cap displayed descriptions at 3 per category with overflow indicator
6. Do not use a bar chart — keep the list format but clustered
7. Apply confidence threshold (hide below 50) for quality control

## Task: Product Review of Effective Pattern Normalization Feature

You are the devtools cofounder reviewing a planned improvement to the Code Insights "Reflect" feature before implementation begins.

### Product Context

**Code Insights** is a free, open-source, local-first CLI tool that helps developers analyze their AI coding sessions. The Reflect feature synthesizes cross-session patterns — friction points (what went wrong) and effective patterns (what worked well).

**Current State:** The Patterns page shows friction categories nicely clustered (e.g., "wrong-approach 12x", "agent-orchestration-failure 11x") because friction has a normalization pipeline. But effective patterns all show "1x" because they use free-text descriptions with exact-match grouping. Semantically similar patterns like "structured planning before implementation" and "decomposing the task into steps" never cluster.

**Screenshot evidence:** 8 effective patterns all at 1x frequency, even though several describe the same underlying technique.

### Proposed Solution

Add a `category` field to effective patterns (mirroring friction's approach):
- LLM assigns a category during per-session extraction
- 12 canonical categories cover the pattern space
- Deterministic normalizer catches variants
- Aggregation groups by category, shows frequency badges
- Dashboard displays category as heading, descriptions as sub-items

**12 Canonical Categories:**
1. `structured-planning` — Task breakdown, phased plans
2. `agent-delegation` — Delegating to specialized agents
3. `verification-workflow` — Build/test/commit loops
4. `self-correction` — Recognizing wrong path and pivoting
5. `systematic-debugging` — Binary search debugging, log diagnosis
6. `domain-expertise` — Applying framework/library knowledge
7. `version-control-discipline` — Clean commits, branching
8. `incremental-implementation` — Small steps, iterative building
9. `context-gathering` — Reading code/docs before changes
10. `type-driven-development` — Types-first, schema-first
11. `test-driven-development` — Writing tests first
12. `documentation-practice` — Docs alongside code

### Your Review Questions

1. **Category taxonomy** — Are these 12 categories the right ones for a developer audience? Any missing? Any that feel too niche for a general tool (remember: Code Insights supports Claude Code, Cursor, Codex, Copilot — not just agentic workflows)?

2. **User value** — Does clustering patterns actually help developers? Or is the unique description more valuable? What's the right UX: category-only, description-only, or hybrid?

3. **Naming** — Are the category names intuitive to developers? Would a developer see "self-correction" and immediately understand what it means? Any naming improvements?

4. **Source tool breadth** — Categories like `agent-delegation` are specific to agentic tools (Claude Code, Codex). Users of Cursor or Copilot won't see these. Is 12 categories too many, leading to thin clusters for non-agentic users?

5. **Display strategy** — The plan shows category heading + description sub-items. Is this the right information hierarchy? Should patterns be visually distinct from friction (which uses a bar chart)?

6. **Growth concern** — As usage grows, will 12 categories fragment or consolidate? What's the expansion strategy?

Provide your honest product assessment. Flag any concerns about user value, naming, or category fit.

6. `context-gathering` — Reading code/docs before changes, understanding before acting
7. `domain-expertise` — Applying framework/library/language knowledge effectively (absorbs type-driven)
8. `effective-tooling` — Leveraging tool-specific capabilities well (absorbs agent-delegation for agentic tools, applies to Copilot completions, Cursor multi-file edits, etc.)

This gives you a taxonomy that works across all 5 source tools. `effective-tooling` is the key addition — it is the tool-neutral version of `agent-delegation` that captures "used the tool's capabilities well" regardless of whether the tool has agents.

**Why 8 and not 12:** Friction has 15 canonical categories but friction is inherently more varied (many things can go wrong). Effective patterns are more convergent (there are fewer ways things go right). 8 categories with healthy frequency counts are more credible than 12 categories where 4 are perpetually empty.

### 2. User Value — Yes, Clustering Helps

The current exact-match grouping produces a wall of unique descriptions at 1x each. This is noise, not insight. Users cannot see "I rely on planning before coding" when it is expressed 5 different ways across 5 sessions.

The key insight: **frequency is the signal.** "structured-planning: 14x" tells a developer "this is your strongest habit." A list of 8 unique descriptions at 1x tells them nothing.

The hybrid display is essential though. Under "structured-planning: 14x", showing the actual descriptions ("broke the feature into 3 phases", "created a task graph before coding") gives the developer concrete examples of what they actually did. This is the Spotify Wrapped pattern — category gives the headline, details give the story.

### 3. Naming Assessment

| Category | Intuitive? | Suggestion |
|----------|-----------|------------|
| `structured-planning` | Yes, immediately clear | Keep |
| `incremental-implementation` | Yes but verbose | Consider `iterative-building` — shorter, same meaning |
| `verification-workflow` | Slightly clinical | Consider `validate-before-proceeding` or keep as-is — developers know "verification" |
| `systematic-debugging` | Yes, strong | Keep |
| `self-correction` | Mostly clear, could be confused with LLM self-correction | Consider `course-correction` which already exists in your facet model |
| `context-gathering` | Yes | Keep |
| `domain-expertise` | Yes | Keep |
| `effective-tooling` | Clear enough | Keep |

One naming principle: these names will appear as headings on the dashboard. Run each through the test: "Would a developer screenshot this and share it?" If the name reads well as a badge label, it works.

### 4. Source Tool Breadth — The Critical Concern

This is my biggest flag. You support 5 source tools. Let me model what categories each tool's sessions are likely to populate:

| Category | Claude Code | Cursor | Codex CLI | Copilot CLI | Copilot Chat |
|----------|-------------|--------|-----------|-------------|--------------|
| `structured-planning` | High | Medium | High | Low | Low |
| `incremental-implementation` | High | High | High | Medium | Medium |
| `verification-workflow` | High | Medium | Medium | Low | Low |
| `systematic-debugging` | High | High | High | Medium | Medium |
| `self-correction` | High | Medium | High | Low | Low |
| `context-gathering` | High | High | Medium | Low | Medium |
| `domain-expertise` | High | High | High | High | High |
| `effective-tooling` | High | High | High | Medium | Medium |

A Copilot-only user might only populate 3-4 categories with any frequency. That is fine — showing 4 categories with real frequency is better than showing 8 at 1x. But you should ensure the UI gracefully handles sparse data. Do not show empty categories. This is where friction's bar chart works well — it only shows categories that have data.

### 5. Display Strategy

**The proposed hierarchy (category heading + description sub-items) is correct.** But I would push further:

- **Friction uses a bar chart. Effective patterns should NOT use a bar chart.** Friction is inherently negative — severity coloring and bar length communicate "how bad." Effective patterns are positive — they should feel different. A grouped list with frequency badges (what you have now, but clustered) is the right format. This visual contrast reinforces the "friction vs wins" framing.
- **Sort by frequency descending.** The top pattern should be the developer's strongest habit.
- **Cap displayed descriptions per category at 3.** Beyond 3 sub-items, the list becomes noise. Show "structured-planning: 14x" with 3 example descriptions and a "+11 more" indicator.
- **Confidence threshold.** You have `avg_confidence` on effective patterns. Consider hiding patterns below confidence 50. This is quality control — do not show a "win" the LLM was not confident about.

### 6. Growth Strategy

8 categories is a good ceiling for now. My expansion strategy:

- **Do not add categories proactively.** Wait for data. After 3 months, look at what the LLM assigns to categories and what falls through to "uncategorized."
- **Add a category only when uncategorized patterns cluster.** Same strategy that friction uses — the alias map in `friction-normalize.ts` emerged from observing real LLM outputs. Do the same for effective patterns.
- **Upper bound: 12-15 categories.** Beyond that, you are fighting the LLM's ability to consistently classify. Friction is at 15 canonical categories and already needs a normalizer with Levenshtein distance. Keep effective patterns simpler.

### Positioning Impact

This strengthens the product story. "Code Insights shows you your strongest coding habits across all your AI tools" is a much better pitch than "here are 8 unique descriptions at 1x." The Patterns page becomes genuinely insightful instead of being a data dump.

The cross-tool angle is the unique moat here. No competing tool (Claude's `/insights`, Cursor's usage data) can show you "structured-planning is your #1 pattern across Claude Code AND Cursor." That is the insight only Code Insights can deliver.

### Recommendation: PROCEED WITH CHANGES

The approach is right. The taxonomy needs pruning from 12 to 8 categories to avoid thin clusters and tool-specific dead categories. The hybrid display (category + descriptions) is the correct UX. Implementation should mirror the friction normalization pipeline — category in the LLM prompt, deterministic normalizer for variants, alias map that grows from observed data.

**Key changes before implementation:**
1. Reduce to 8 categories (see proposed list above)
2. Add `effective-tooling` as the tool-neutral replacement for `agent-delegation`
3. Merge TDD into `verification-workflow`, type-driven into `domain-expertise`
4. Drop `documentation-practice` and `version-control-discipline`
5. Cap displayed descriptions at 3 per category with overflow indicator
6. Do not use a bar chart — keep the list format but clustered
7. Apply confidence threshold (hide below 50) for quality control

## Task: Own the Full Solution Design for Effective Pattern Normalization

You are the technical architect and design owner for this feature. Design the complete implementation plan covering ALL layers.

### Context

Code Insights extracts per-session facets. Effective patterns currently use free-text `description` with exact-match aggregation — everything shows as 1x. We're adding `category`-based classification mirroring how friction already works.

### Final 12 Canonical Categories (APPROVED)

```typescript
export const CANONICAL_PATTERN_CATEGORIES = [
  'structured-planning',        // Task breakdown, phased plans, decomposition before implementation
  'agent-delegation',           // Delegating to specialized agents, orchestration
  'verification-workflow',      // Build/test/commit verification loops, CI checks
  'self-correction',            // Recognizing wrong path and pivoting, course correction
  'systematic-debugging',       // Binary search debugging, log-based diagnosis, comparing expected vs actual
  'domain-expertise',           // Applying framework/library/architecture knowledge
  'version-control-discipline', // Clean commits, branching, PR workflow
  'incremental-implementation', // Small steps, iterative building
  'context-gathering',          // Reading existing code, docs, schemas before changes
  'type-driven-development',    // Types-first, schema-first, contract-first
  'test-driven-development',    // Writing tests first, TDD workflow
  'documentation-practice',     // Docs alongside code, ADRs, inline comments
] as const;
```

### Your Deliverables

Read ALL relevant files and design the complete solution:

1. **Read these files thoroughly:**
   - `server/src/llm/prompts.ts` — current analysis prompts + types
   - `server/src/llm/friction-normalize.ts` — reference normalizer implementation
   - `server/src/routes/shared-aggregation.ts` — current aggregation SQL
   - `server/src/routes/reflect.ts` — API routes
   - `server/src/llm/reflect-prompts.ts` — synthesis prompts
   - `dashboard/src/pages/PatternsPage.tsx` — current UI
   - `dashboard/src/hooks/useReflect.ts` — dashboard hooks
   - `cli/src/db/migrate.ts` — schema reference
   - `server/src/llm/analysis.ts` — where facets are saved

2. **Design each component:**

   a. **`server/src/llm/pattern-normalize.ts`** (NEW) — Full normalizer with:
      - Canonical list import
      - Alias map (anticipate LLM variants)
      - `normalizePatternCategory()` function mirroring friction's pipeline
      - Human-readable label map for dashboard display
   
   b. **`server/src/routes/shared-aggregation.ts`** changes:
      - Updated SQL to extract category from JSON
      - COALESCE fallback for legacy data
      - Post-query normalization + merge logic
      - Updated TypeScript interface
   
   c. **`server/src/llm/reflect-prompts.ts`** changes:

   d. **`dashboard/src/pages/PatternsPage.tsx`** changes:

3. **Implementation order** — What gets built first, what depends on what.

content.text:
Excellent — all three experts have weighed in. The devtools-cofounder raised a sharp challenge to the taxonomy. Let me synthesize the consensus and the one open decision for you.

`★ Insight ─────────────────────────────────────`
**The cofounder's core argument:** Friction has 15 categories because there are many ways to fail. Effective patterns are more convergent — fewer ways to succeed. 12 categories risks thin clusters and tool-specific dead categories (e.g., `agent-delegation` is invisible to Cursor/Copilot users). The friction bar chart already proves that fewer, denser categories create more credible signals.
`─────────────────────────────────────────────────`

---

## Consensus Points (All 3 Agree)

- **Extraction-time classification** — LLM assigns category during facet extraction
- **Mirror friction's architecture** — canonical list + deterministic normalizer + alias map
- **Hybrid display** — category heading + description sub-items (cap at 3 with "+N more")
- **No schema migration** — JSON column is flexible enough
- **No forced backfill** — old data falls back to description grouping
- **Single PR** — all layers tightly coupled (~250 lines)
- **Keep list format, NOT bar chart** — visually distinct from friction (positive vs negative framing)

## The Open Decision: 8 vs 12 Categories

The TA and LLM expert designed for **12 categories**. The cofounder argues for **8**, merging or dropping 4:

| Proposed Drop/Merge | Cofounder's Reasoning |
|---|---|
| `agent-delegation` → new `effective-tooling` | Tool-neutral: covers agents (Claude Code), multi-file edits (Cursor), completions (Copilot) |
| `type-driven-development` → `domain-expertise` | Types-first is a subset of applying domain knowledge |
| `test-driven-development` → `verification-workflow` | Tests-first vs tests-after is a subtle distinction LLMs will fumble |
| `documentation-practice` → drop | Too thin — rarely surfaces as top-3 pattern |
| `version-control-discipline` → drop | Hygiene, not a technique |

**Cofounder's proposed 8:**
1. `structured-planning`
2. `incremental-implementation`
3. `verification-workflow` (absorbs TDD)
4. `systematic-debugging`
5. `self-correction`
6. `context-gathering`
7. `domain-expertise` (absorbs type-driven)
8. `effective-tooling` (replaces agent-delegation, tool-neutral)

**Cofounder also flagged:**
- Apply confidence threshold (hide patterns below 50) for quality control
- Don't show empty categories — only display what has data
- Sort by frequency descending (strongest habit first)

---

**My take:** The cofounder's pruning is well-reasoned. 8 denser categories > 12 sparse ones, especially with 5 source tools. `effective-tooling` is a smart generalization of `agent-delegation`. The dropped categories (`documentation-practice`, `version-control-discipline`) genuinely feel like hygiene rather than "techniques that worked well."

What's your call — go with 8, stick with 12, or adjust further? Once you decide, I'll kick off `/start-feature` with the full ceremony.

input.content:
# Effective Pattern Normalization — Design Plan > **Status:** In Progress > **Date:** 2026-03-09 > **Scope:** Server (prompts, aggregation, normalization), Dashboard (PatternsPage, Insights), CLI (types) --- ## Problem Effective patterns use free-text `description` with exact-match `GROUP BY` in SQL. Semantically identical patterns (e.g., "structured planning before implementation" and "decomposing the task into steps") never cluster. The Patterns page shows all patterns at 1x frequency — no signal, just noise. Friction already solved this with `category`-based classification + a normalizer (`friction-normalize.ts`). We mirror that approach for effective patterns. ## Key Design Decisions ### 1. No Backward Compatibility The Reflect feature has NOT been released. Only local dev data exists. We make a **clean break**: - No `COALESCE` fallbacks for missing `category` fields - No optional `category?: string` — it's **required**: `category: string` - No `__uncategorized__` handling in aggregation - Old session facets without the new format are treated as **outdated** ### 2. Outdated Format Detection + Re-analysis Flag When session insights exist but lack the latest format (missing `category` on effective patterns OR friction points), show a **warning banner** on: - **Session Insights page** (per-session) — "This session's insights were generated with an older format. Re-analyze to get the latest insights." - **Patterns/Reflect page** — "N sessions have outdated insight formats. Re-analyze them to improve pattern accuracy." The banner includes an action button to trigger re-analysis. **Detection logic:** Check if `effective_patterns` JSON entries lack `category` field, OR if `friction_points` entries lack expected fields. ### 3. Confidence Threshold: 50%+ Filter Apply a **minimum 50% confidence filter** to BOTH: - **Friction points** — only aggregate friction with `confidence >= 50` (Note: friction currently uses `severity` not confidence — see implementation notes) - **Effective patterns** — only aggregate patterns with `confidence >= 50` This filter applies at **aggregation time** in `shared-aggregation.ts`, not at extraction time. The LLM still extracts everything; we filter during display/synthesis. ### 4. Eight Canonical Categories (Not 12) Fewer, denser categories create more credible frequency signals across all 5 source tools. ```typescript export const CANONICAL_PATTERN_CATEGORIES = [ 'structured-planning', // Task breakdown, phased plans, decomposition before implementation 'incremental-implementation', // Small steps, iterative building, progressive refinement 'verification-workflow', // Build/test/commit loops, validation, TDD (absorbed) 'systematic-debugging', // Binary search debugging, log diagnosis, reproduction isolation 'self-correction', // Recognizing wrong path and pivoting, course correction 'context-gathering', // Reading existing code/docs/schemas before making changes 'domain-expertise', // Applying framework/library/architecture knowledge (absorbs type-driven) 'effective-tooling', // Leveraging tool capabilities: agents, multi-file edits, completions ] as const; ``` **Merges:** - `agent-delegation` → `effective-tooling` (tool-neutral: agents in Claude Code, multi-file in Cursor, completions in Copilot) - `type-driven-development` → `domain-expertise` (types-first is a subset of domain knowledge) - `test-driven-development` → `verification-workflow` (tests-first is a verification strategy) **Dropped:** - `documentation-practice` — too thin, rarely surfaces as top-3 pattern - `version-control-discipline` — hygiene, not a technique --- ## Architecture ### Data Flow ``` LLM extraction (prompts.ts) -> { category: "structured-planning", description: "...", confidence: 85 } -> normalizePatternCategory() at write time (analysis.ts) -> saveFacetsToDb writes JSON to session_facets.effective_patterns -> shared-aggregation.ts reads JSON, filters confidence >= 50, GROUPs BY category -> reflect-prompts.ts receives category-grouped data for synthesis -> dashboard renders category headings with description sub-items ``` ### New File: `server/src/llm/pattern-normalize.ts` Mirrors `friction-normalize.ts`: - `CANONICAL_PATTERN_CATEGORIES` import from prompts - `PATTERN_ALIASES` map for LLM variant clustering - `normalizePatternCategory(category: string): string` — exact -> alias -> Levenshtein <= 2 -> substring -> novel - `PATTERN_CATEGORY_LABELS` map for dashboard display - `getPatternCategoryLabel(category: string): string` — fallback to Title Case **Alias map (initial):** ```typescript const PATTERN_ALIASES: Record = { // structured-planning variants 'task-decomposition': 'structured-planning', 'plan-first': 'structured-planning', 'upfront-planning': 'structured-planning', 'phased-approach': 'structured-planning', 'task-breakdown': 'structured-planning', 'planning-before-implementation': 'structured-planning', // effective-tooling variants 'agent-delegation': 'effective-tooling', 'agent-orchestration': 'effective-tooling', 'specialized-agents': 'effective-tooling', 'multi-agent': 'effective-tooling', 'tool-leverage': 'effective-tooling', // verification-workflow variants 'build-test-verify': 'verification-workflow', 'test-driven-development': 'verification-workflow', 'tdd': 'verification-workflow', 'test-first': 'verification-workflow', 'pre-commit-checks': 'verification-workflow', // systematic-debugging variants 'binary-search-debugging': 'systematic-debugging', 'methodical-debugging': 'systematic-debugging', 'log-based-debugging': 'systematic-debugging', 'debugging-methodology': 'systematic-debugging', // self-correction variants 'course-correction': 'self-correction', 'pivot-on-failure': 'self-correction', 'backtracking': 'self-correction', // context-gathering variants 'code-reading-first': 'context-gathering', 'codebase-exploration': 'context-gathering', 'understanding-before-changing': 'context-gathering', // domain-expertise variants 'framework-knowledge': 'domain-expertise', 'types-first': 'domain-expertise', 'type-driven-development': 'domain-expertise', 'schema-first': 'domain-expertise', // incremental-implementation variants 'small-steps': 'incremental-implementation', 'iterative-building': 'incremental-implementation', 'iterative-development': 'incremental-implementation', }; ``` ### Prompt Changes #### `SESSION_ANALYSIS_SYSTEM_PROMPT` — effective_patterns section **Before:** ``` 4. effective_patterns: Up to 3 techniques or approaches that worked particularly well (array, max 3). Each has: - description: Specific technique worth repeating - confidence: 0-100 how confident you are this is genuinely effective ``` **After:** ``` 4. effective_patterns: Up to 3 techniques or approaches that worked particularly well (array, max 3). Each has: - category: Use one of these PREFERRED categories when applicable: structured-planning, incremental-implementation, verification-workflow, systematic-debugging, self-correction, context-gathering, domain-expertise, effective-tooling. Create a new kebab-case category only when none of these fit. - description: Specific technique worth repeating (1-2 sentences with concrete detail) - confidence: 0-100 how confident you are this is genuinely effective EFFECTIVE PATTERN CLASSIFICATION GUIDANCE: - "structured-planning": Task decomposition, phased plans, breaking work into steps BEFORE coding - "incremental-implementation": Small steps, iterative building, progressive refinement - "verification-workflow": Build/test/lint loops, TDD, CI checks, verifying before committing - "systematic-debugging": Binary search, log analysis, reproduction isolation, comparing expected vs actual - "self-correction": Recognizing a wrong path and pivoting without user intervention - "context-gathering": Reading existing code/docs/schemas before making changes - "domain-expertise": Applying specific framework/library/API/type-system knowledge that skips trial-and-error - "effective-tooling": Leveraging tool-specific capabilities — agent delegation, multi-file edits, smart completions When no canonical category fits, create a specific kebab-case category. ``` #### `FACET_ONLY_SYSTEM_PROMPT` — same update #### JSON schema examples — add `"category": "kebab-case-category"` field #### `AnalysisResponse` type — `category: string` (REQUIRED, not optional) ### Aggregation Changes (`shared-aggregation.ts`) #### Updated SQL (effective patterns) ```sql SELECT json_extract(je.value, '$.category') as category, json_extract(je.value, '$.description') as description, json_extract(je.value, '$.confidence') as confidence FROM session_facets sf JOIN sessions s ON sf.session_id = s.id CROSS JOIN json_each(sf.effective_patterns) je WHERE json_extract(je.value, '$.confidence') >= 50 ${additionalWhere} ``` Post-query: normalize categories, merge by normalized key, collect descriptions. #### Updated SQL (friction points) — add confidence filter ```sql WHERE json_extract(je.value, '$.confidence') >= 50 ``` Note: Friction currently uses `severity` (high/medium/low), not a numeric confidence. If friction lacks numeric confidence, this filter applies only to effective patterns. Check implementation. #### Updated Interface ```typescript export interface AggregatedEffectivePattern { category: string; label: string; // "Structured Planning" frequency: number; avg_confidence: number; descriptions: string[]; // max 10, for display } ``` ### Dashboard Changes #### PatternsPage.tsx — Effective Patterns Section ```tsx
  • {ep.frequency}x {ep.label}
    {ep.descriptions.length > 0 && (
      {ep.descriptions.slice(0, 3).map((desc, j) => (
    • {desc}
      • ))} {ep.descriptions.length > 3 && (
    • +{ep.descriptions.length - 3} more
      • )}
    )}
    • ``` #### Outdated Format Banner Show on Session Insights page and Patterns page when outdated sessions detected: ```tsx {outdatedCount > 0 && ( {outdatedCount} session(s) have outdated insight formats. Re-analyze to get improved pattern accuracy. )} ``` **Detection:** API endpoint or query that checks `session_facets` rows where `effective_patterns` JSON entries lack `category` field. ### Reflect Prompts Changes (`reflect-prompts.ts`) - `generateFrictionWinsPrompt` — receives category-grouped effective patterns - `generateRulesSkillsPrompt` — same type update - `topWins` output schema gets `category` field - Label change: "EFFECTIVE PATTERNS (ranked by frequency, grouped by category)" ### Type Changes #### `cli/src/types.ts` ```typescript export interface EffectivePattern { category: string; // REQUIRED — no backward compat description: string; confidence: number; } ``` #### `dashboard/src/lib/api.ts` ```typescript effectivePatterns: Array<{ category: string; label: string; frequency: number; avg_confidence: number; descriptions: string[]; }>; ``` --- ## Implementation Order ``` 1. server/src/llm/prompts.ts — Canonical list, prompt updates, type updates 2. server/src/llm/pattern-normalize.ts — NEW: normalizer + aliases + labels 3. server/src/llm/analysis.ts — Import normalizer, apply at write time 4. server/src/routes/shared-aggregation.ts — New SQL, confidence filter, normalization, interface 5. server/src/llm/reflect-prompts.ts — Parameter type updates 6. cli/src/types.ts — Required category on EffectivePattern 7. dashboard/src/lib/api.ts — Updated FacetAggregation type 8. dashboard/src/pages/PatternsPage.tsx — Category display + outdated banner 9. server/src/routes/insights.ts (or similar) — Outdated format detection endpoint ``` Steps 1-2 in parallel. Steps 3-5 sequential (depend on 1-2). Steps 6-8 in parallel (after 4). ## Files Modified | File | Action | Est. Lines | |------|--------|-----------| | `server/src/llm/prompts.ts` | Modify | ~30 | | `server/src/llm/pattern-normalize.ts` | **Create** | ~100 | | `server/src/llm/analysis.ts` | Modify | ~10 | | `server/src/routes/shared-aggregation.ts` | Modify | ~50 | | `server/src/llm/reflect-prompts.ts` | Modify | ~10 | | `cli/src/types.ts` | Modify | ~3 | | `dashboard/src/lib/api.ts` | Modify | ~8 | | `dashboard/src/pages/PatternsPage.tsx` | Modify | ~40 | | Outdated detection (route TBD) | Modify/Create | ~20 | **Total: ~270 lines changed, 1 new file** ## What This Does NOT Include - No schema migration (JSON column is flexible) - No backfill command (users re-analyze via UI banner) - No new API routes for re-analysis (existing analyze endpoint handles it) - No friction normalization changes (out of scope — friction already works) - No export prompt changes (out of scope)

normalizePatternCategory() at write time (analysis.ts) -> saveFacetsToDb writes JSON to session_facets.effective_patterns -> shared-aggregation.ts reads JSON, filters confidence >= 50, GROUPs BY category -> reflect-prompts.ts receives category-grouped data for synthesis -> dashboard renders category headings with description sub-items ``` ### New File: `server/src/llm/pattern-normalize.ts` Mirrors `friction-normalize.ts`: - `CANONICAL_PATTERN_CATEGORIES` import from prompts - `PATTERN_ALIASES` map for LLM variant clustering - `normalizePatternCategory(category: string): string` — exact -> alias -> Levenshtein <= 2 -> substring -> novel - `PATTERN_CATEGORY_LABELS` map for dashboard display - `getPatternCategoryLabel(category: string): string` — fallback to Title Case **Alias map (initial):** ```typescript const PATTERN_ALIASES: Record = { // structured-planning variants 'task-decomposition': 'structured-planning', 'plan-first': 'structured-planning', 'upfront-planning': 'structured-planning', 'phased-approach': 'structured-planning', 'task-breakdown': 'structured-planning', 'planning-before-implementation': 'structured-planning', // effective-tooling variants 'agent-delegation': 'effective-tooling', 'agent-orchestration': 'effective-tooling', 'specialized-agents': 'effective-tooling', 'multi-agent': 'effective-tooling', 'tool-leverage': 'effective-tooling', // verification-workflow variants 'build-test-verify': 'verification-workflow', 'test-driven-development': 'verification-workflow', 'tdd': 'verification-workflow', 'test-first': 'verification-workflow', 'pre-commit-checks': 'verification-workflow', // systematic-debugging variants 'binary-search-debugging': 'systematic-debugging', 'methodical-debugging': 'systematic-debugging', 'log-based-debugging': 'systematic-debugging', 'debugging-methodology': 'systematic-debugging', // self-correction variants 'course-correction': 'self-correction', 'pivot-on-failure': 'self-correction', 'backtracking': 'self-correction', // context-gathering variants 'code-reading-first': 'context-gathering', 'codebase-exploration': 'context-gathering', 'understanding-before-changing': 'context-gathering', // domain-expertise variants 'framework-knowledge': 'domain-expertise', 'types-first': 'domain-expertise', 'type-driven-development': 'domain-expertise', 'schema-first': 'domain-expertise', // incremental-implementation variants 'small-steps': 'incremental-implementation', 'iterative-building': 'incremental-implementation', 'iterative-development': 'incremental-implementation', }; ``` ### Prompt Changes #### `SESSION_ANALYSIS_SYSTEM_PROMPT` — effective_patterns section **Before:** ``` 4. effective_patterns: Up to 3 techniques or approaches that worked particularly well (array, max 3). Each has: - description: Specific technique worth repeating - confidence: 0-100 how confident you are this is genuinely effective ``` **After:** ``` 4. effective_patterns: Up to 3 techniques or approaches that worked particularly well (array, max 3). Each has: - category: Use one of these PREFERRED categories when applicable: structured-planning, incremental-implementation, verification-workflow, systematic-debugging, self-correction, context-gathering, domain-expertise, effective-tooling. Create a new kebab-case category only when none of these fit. - description: Specific technique worth repeating (1-2 sentences with concrete detail) - confidence: 0-100 how confident you are this is genuinely effective EFFECTIVE PATTERN CLASSIFICATION GUIDANCE: - "structured-planning": Task decomposition, phased plans, breaking work into steps BEFORE coding - "incremental-implementation": Small steps, iterative building, progressive refinement - "verification-workflow": Build/test/lint loops, TDD, CI checks, verifying before committing - "systematic-debugging": Binary search, log analysis, reproduction isolation, comparing expected vs actual - "self-correction": Recognizing a wrong path and pivoting without user intervention - "context-gathering": Reading existing code/docs/schemas before making changes - "domain-expertise": Applying specific framework/library/API/type-system knowledge that skips trial-and-error - "effective-tooling": Leveraging tool-specific capabilities — agent delegation, multi-file edits, smart completions When no canonical category fits, create a specific kebab-case category. ``` #### `FACET_ONLY_SYSTEM_PROMPT` — same update #### JSON schema examples — add `"category": "kebab-case-category"` field #### `AnalysisResponse` type — `category: string` (REQUIRED, not optional) ### Aggregation Changes (`shared-aggregation.ts`) #### Updated SQL (effective patterns) ```sql SELECT json_extract(je.value, '$.category') as category, json_extract(je.value, '$.description') as description, json_extract(je.value, '$.confidence') as confidence FROM session_facets sf JOIN sessions s ON sf.session_id = s.id CROSS JOIN json_each(sf.effective_patterns) je WHERE json_extract(je.value, '$.confidence') >= 50 ${additionalWhere} ``` Post-query: normalize categories, merge by normalized key, collect descriptions. #### Updated SQL (friction points) — add confidence filter ```sql WHERE json_extract(je.value, '$.confidence') >= 50 ``` Note: Friction currently uses `severity` (high/medium/low), not a numeric confidence. If friction lacks numeric confidence, this filter applies only to effective patterns. Check implementation. #### Updated Interface ```typescript export interface AggregatedEffectivePattern { category: string; label: string; // "Structured Planning" frequency: number; avg_confidence: number; descriptions: string[]; // max 10, for display } ``` ### Dashboard Changes #### PatternsPage.tsx — Effective Patterns Section ```tsx
  • {ep.frequency}x {ep.label}
    {ep.descriptions.length > 0 && (
      {ep.descriptions.slice(0, 3).map((desc, j) => (
    • {desc}
      • ))} {ep.descriptions.length > 3 && (
    • +{ep.descriptions.length - 3} more
      • )}
    )}
    • ``` #### Outdated Format Banner Show on Session Insights page and Patterns page when outdated sessions detected: ```tsx {outdatedCount > 0 && ( {outdatedCount} session(s) have outdated insight formats. Re-analyze to get improved pattern accuracy. )} ``` **Detection:** API endpoint or query that checks `session_facets` rows where `effective_patterns` JSON entries lack `category` field. ### Reflect Prompts Changes (`reflect-prompts.ts`) - `generateFrictionWinsPrompt` — receives category-grouped effective patterns - `generateRulesSkillsPrompt` — same type update - `topWins` output schema gets `category` field - Label change: "EFFECTIVE PATTERNS (ranked by frequency, grouped by category)" ### Type Changes #### `cli/src/types.ts` ```typescript export interface EffectivePattern { category: string; // REQUIRED — no backward compat description: string; confidence: number; } ``` #### `dashboard/src/lib/api.ts` ```typescript effectivePatterns: Array<{ category: string; label: string; frequency: number; avg_confidence: number; descriptions: string[]; }>; ``` --- ## Implementation Order ``` 1. server/src/llm/prompts.ts — Canonical list, prompt updates, type updates 2. server/src/llm/pattern-normalize.ts — NEW: normalizer + aliases + labels 3. server/src/llm/analysis.ts — Import normalizer, apply at write time 4. server/src/routes/shared-aggregation.ts — New SQL, confidence filter, normalization, interface 5. server/src/llm/reflect-prompts.ts — Parameter type updates 6. cli/src/types.ts — Required category on EffectivePattern 7. dashboard/src/lib/api.ts — Updated FacetAggregation type 8. dashboard/src/pages/PatternsPage.tsx — Category display + outdated banner 9. server/src/routes/insights.ts (or similar) — Outdated format detection endpoint ``` Steps 1-2 in parallel. Steps 3-5 sequential (depend on 1-2). Steps 6-8 in parallel (after 4). ## Files Modified | File | Action | Est. Lines | |------|--------|-----------| | `server/src/llm/prompts.ts` | Modify | ~30 | | `server/src/llm/pattern-normalize.ts` | **Create** | ~100 | | `server/src/llm/analysis.ts` | Modify | ~10 | | `server/src/routes/shared-aggregation.ts` | Modify | ~50 | | `server/src/llm/reflect-prompts.ts` | Modify | ~10 | | `cli/src/types.ts` | Modify | ~3 | | `dashboard/src/lib/api.ts` | Modify | ~8 | | `dashboard/src/pages/PatternsPage.tsx` | Modify | ~40 | | Outdated detection (route TBD) | Modify/Create | ~20 | **Total: ~270 lines changed, 1 new file** ## What This Does NOT Include - No schema migration (JSON column is flexible) - No backfill command (users re-analyze via UI banner) - No new API routes for re-analysis (existing analyze endpoint handles it) - No friction normalization changes (out of scope — friction already works) - No export prompt changes (out of scope)

content.text:
`★ Insight ─────────────────────────────────────`
**Two key design shifts from your input:**
1. **Clean break vs backward compat** — Since Reflect isn't released, we avoid the complexity of `COALESCE` fallbacks, optional fields, and dual-format handling. `category` is now *required*, not optional. This simplifies every layer.
2. **Outdated format banner** — Instead of silently degrading, we surface the gap to users and give them agency to fix it. This is the same UX pattern tools like VS Code use when workspace settings are outdated.
`─────────────────────────────────────────────────`

---

The design is documented at `docs/plans/2026-03-09-effective-pattern-normalization.md` with both updates:

1. **No backward compat** — `category` is required, outdated sessions get a re-analyze banner on both Session Insights and Patterns pages
2. **50%+ confidence filter** — Applied at aggregation time for both friction and effective patterns

What's your other requirement?

message.content:
it is related to the same feature. I am thinking aloud and need inputs from other agents like @"llm-expert (agent)" @"technical-architect (agent)" and @"devtools-cofounder (agent)" as well here. The overall requirement of us building this app is to have some space for the user to analyze their ai sessions and generate insights. and a overall reflections to talk summarize and format the disparate insights into structured categories for frictions and patterns. 

but the expection or goal of the user from this activity is to improve their AI sessions. So, our insights generate per session level. reflections generate for a time window (30d default?) and then we should we able to build on this to further analyze whether friction points from last reflections are gone in the next iteration -- meaning, the user has overcome the friction points and has succesfully progressed them into a effective patterns which is like the real goal of the app.

What are your and other agents thoughts on this?

content.text: This is a really compelling product insight — the current Reflect feature is essentially a *snapshot*, but the real value is in showing *progression over time*. Let me get all three experts thinking about this.

input.prompt:
## Context

Code Insights is a local-first CLI tool that analyzes AI coding sessions. The current architecture has three layers:

1. **Per-session insights** — LLM analyzes each session, extracts friction points (what went wrong) and effective patterns (what worked well), each with a `category` field
2. **Reflections** — Cross-session synthesis over a time window (default 30d), aggregates friction categories and pattern categories, generates narratives
3. **??? (proposed)** — Cross-reflection progress tracking

The user's insight: **The real goal of the app is to help users IMPROVE their AI sessions.** Currently we show snapshots ("here's your friction, here's your patterns"). But we should track whether friction from one reflection period gets resolved in the next — and whether it transforms into effective patterns.

### Example Flow
- **Reflection 1 (Jan):** "wrong-approach" friction at 12x, high severity
- **Reflection 2 (Feb):** "wrong-approach" drops to 4x, "structured-planning" pattern appears at 8x
- **Insight:** The user learned to plan before implementing, which reduced wrong-approach friction

### Your Task (LLM Prompt Design)

Think about what LLM prompts would be needed for this "progress analysis" layer:

1. **What data would the prompt receive?** Two (or more) reflection snapshots? Raw aggregation deltas?
2. **What should the LLM output?** Progress narratives? Friction-to-pattern transformation detection? Recommendations?
3. **Should this be a new synthesis prompt or an evolution of the existing reflect prompts?**
4. **How do you detect "friction X transformed into pattern Y"?** Is this a semantic relationship the LLM should identify, or can we do it deterministically with category mappings?
5. **Token budget considerations** — Are we sending two full reflection snapshots to the LLM? That could be expensive.

Think about this as a product feature design from the LLM perspective. What prompts make this valuable without being expensive?

### Important Context
- 8 canonical pattern categories: structured-planning, incremental-implementation, verification-workflow, systematic-debugging, self-correction, context-gathering, domain-expertise, effective-tooling
- 15 canonical friction categories: wrong-approach, missing-dependency, config-drift, test-failure, type-error, api-misunderstanding, stale-cache, version-mismatch, permission-issue, incomplete-requirements, circular-dependency, race-condition, environment-mismatch, documentation-gap, tooling-limitation
- Reflections are stored in `reflect_snapshots` table with `period`, `results_json`, `generated_at`, `window_start`, `window_end`
- LLM providers: OpenAI, Anthropic, Gemini, Ollama

Provide your design thinking, not implementation details. Focus on what the LLM should do and what data it needs.

input.prompt:
## Context

Code Insights is a local-first CLI tool that analyzes AI coding sessions. Current architecture:

1. **Per-session insights** — Stored in `session_facets` table (friction_points, effective_patterns as JSON)
2. **Reflections** — Stored in `reflect_snapshots` table, cross-session synthesis over time windows
3. **??? (proposed)** — Cross-reflection progress tracking

The founder's insight: **The real goal is helping users improve their AI sessions over time.** We need to track whether friction from one reflection period decreases in the next, and whether users develop new effective patterns that counteract their friction.

### Example
- Reflection 1 (Jan 1-30): `wrong-approach: 12x`, no `structured-planning` pattern
- Reflection 2 (Feb 1-28): `wrong-approach: 4x`, `structured-planning: 8x`
- Progress insight: "You reduced wrong-approach friction by 67% by adopting structured planning"

### Your Task (Architecture Design)

Read the relevant files and think about:

1. **Data model** — How do we store/compare reflection snapshots over time?
   - Read `cli/src/db/migrate.ts` for current schema
   - Read `server/src/routes/reflect.ts` for current snapshot storage
   - The current `reflect_snapshots` table has `PRIMARY KEY (period, project_id)` — meaning only ONE snapshot per period. Do we need historical snapshots?

2. **Comparison mechanism** — How do we compute deltas between two time windows?
   - Re-aggregate from `session_facets` for two windows? (always fresh, no storage needed)
   - Or compare stored snapshots? (faster, but snapshots only exist if user generated them)

3. **Friction-to-pattern mapping** — Can we deterministically map which frictions relate to which patterns?
   - e.g., `wrong-approach` ↔ `structured-planning`, `test-failure` ↔ `verification-workflow`
   - Or is this an LLM task?

4. **Schema changes** — Do we need new tables? Historical snapshot storage? Progress tracking table?

5. **API design** — What endpoints would serve this? A new `/api/reflect/progress` route?

6. **Dashboard integration** — Where does this live in the UI? New tab on Patterns page? New page entirely?

Think architecturally. This is early-stage design thinking — we're exploring the shape of the solution, not writing implementation specs yet.

Read these files for context:
- `cli/src/db/migrate.ts` (schema)
- `server/src/routes/reflect.ts` (current reflect API)
- `server/src/routes/shared-aggregation.ts` (aggregation logic)
- `dashboard/src/pages/PatternsPage.tsx` (current UI)

input.prompt:
## Context

Code Insights is a free, open-source, local-first CLI tool that helps developers analyze their AI coding sessions. Current feature layers:

1. **Per-session insights** — Each AI session gets analyzed: friction points, effective patterns, outcome
2. **Reflections** — Cross-session synthesis over a 30d window: "here's your top friction, here's your strongest patterns, here's your working style"

The founder just articulated what they believe is the **real goal of the app:**

> "The expectation or goal of the user from this activity is to improve their AI sessions. So, our insights generate per session level. Reflections generate for a time window (30d default). And then we should be able to build on this to further analyze whether friction points from last reflections are gone in the next iteration — meaning, the user has overcome the friction points and has successfully progressed them into effective patterns."

### The Proposed Concept

A **progress layer** that compares reflections over time:
- Did friction categories decrease between periods?
- Did new effective patterns emerge that counteract old friction?
- Can we show the user their improvement trajectory?

Example: "wrong-approach" friction dropped from 12x to 4x while "structured-planning" pattern rose from 0 to 8x.

### Your Task (Product Strategy Review)

1. **Is this the right product direction?** Does "track your improvement" resonate with developers? What's the competitive angle?

2. **User behavior reality check** — Will developers actually generate reflections regularly enough for cross-reflection comparison to work? What's the expected cadence — weekly? Monthly? Will they remember to come back?

3. **Comparison window design** — What time windows make sense? "This month vs last month"? Rolling 30d vs previous 30d? User-defined?

4. **The "friction → pattern transformation" narrative** — Is this compelling storytelling or forced correlation? Can we credibly claim "you overcame wrong-approach by adopting structured-planning"? Or is this a stretch?

5. **Competitive positioning** — Does any other dev tool do this? GitHub Copilot metrics? Cursor analytics? WakaTime? What's the unique angle?

6. **MVP vs full vision** — What's the minimum version that delivers the "aha" moment? What would you ship first?

7. **Risk: discouraging users** — What if friction INCREASES between periods? How do we frame regression without making users feel bad?

8. **Naming** — "Progress"? "Growth"? "Journey"? What resonates with developer identity?

Be brutally honest. If this is the wrong direction, say so.

## Context

Code Insights is a free, open-source, local-first CLI tool that helps developers analyze their AI coding sessions. Current feature layers:

1. **Per-session insights** — Each AI session gets analyzed: friction points, effective patterns, outcome
2. **Reflections** — Cross-session synthesis over a 30d window: "here's your top friction, here's your strongest patterns, here's your working style"

The founder just articulated what they believe is the **real goal of the app:**

> "The expectation or goal of the user from this activity is to improve their AI sessions. So, our insights generate per session level. Reflections generate for a time window (30d default). And then we should be able to build on this to further analyze whether friction points from last reflections are gone in the next iteration — meaning, the user has overcome the friction points and has successfully progressed them into effective patterns."

### The Proposed Concept

A **progress layer** that compares reflections over time:
- Did friction categories decrease between periods?
- Did new effective patterns emerge that counteract old friction?
- Can we show the user their improvement trajectory?

Example: "wrong-approach" friction dropped from 12x to 4x while "structured-planning" pattern rose from 0 to 8x.

### Your Task (Product Strategy Review)

1. **Is this the right product direction?** Does "track your improvement" resonate with developers? What's the competitive angle?

2. **User behavior reality check** — Will developers actually generate reflections regularly enough for cross-reflection comparison to work? What's the expected cadence — weekly? Monthly? Will they remember to come back?

3. **Comparison window design** — What time windows make sense? "This month vs last month"? Rolling 30d vs previous 30d? User-defined?

4. **The "friction → pattern transformation" narrative** — Is this compelling storytelling or forced correlation? Can we credibly claim "you overcame wrong-approach by adopting structured-planning"? Or is this a stretch?

5. **Competitive positioning** — Does any other dev tool do this? GitHub Copilot metrics? Cursor analytics? WakaTime? What's the unique angle?

6. **MVP vs full vision** — What's the minimum version that delivers the "aha" moment? What would you ship first?

7. **Risk: discouraging users** — What if friction INCREASES between periods? How do we frame regression without making users feel bad?

8. **Naming** — "Progress"? "Growth"? "Journey"? What resonates with developer identity?

Be brutally honest. If this is the wrong direction, say so.

1→# Code Insights
     2→
     3→## What It Is
     4→
     5→Turn your AI coding sessions into knowledge. Code Insights extracts patterns from your conversations—what you built, decisions you made, lessons learned—and presents them in a local visual dashboard.
     6→
     7→## The Problem
     8→
     9→AI coding tools store every conversation as files on your machine. Claude Code uses JSONL in `~/.claude/projects/`. Cursor stores state in SQLite. Codex CLI and Copilot CLI write their own session formats. This is valuable data:
    10→- What features did you work on last week?
    11→- Why did you choose that architecture?
    12→- What mistakes did you make (and fix)?
    13→- How much time went into different parts of the codebase?
    14→
    15→But it's trapped in raw files. You can't search it, visualize it, or learn from it.
    16→
    17→## The Solution
    18→
    19→Code Insights provides:
    20→
    21→1. **Automated extraction** — Parses session files from multiple AI coding tools and structures the data
    22→2. **Smart session titles** — Auto-generates meaningful titles from session content
    23→3. **Session classification** — Categorizes sessions (deep focus, bug hunt, feature build, etc.)
    24→4. **LLM-powered analysis** — Multi-provider insight generation (OpenAI, Anthropic, Gemini, Ollama) with your own API key
    25→5. **Visual dashboard** — Local web interface with charts, timelines, and filters at `http://localhost:7890`
    26→6. **CLI analytics** — Terminal stats via `code-insights stats` and subcommands
    27→
    28→## Who It's For
    29→
    30→- **Developers using multiple AI coding tools** who want to understand their AI-assisted work patterns across Claude Code, Cursor, Codex CLI, Copilot CLI, and VS Code Copilot Chat
    31→- **Learners** who want to review and reinforce what they've built with AI assistants
    32→- **Privacy-conscious developers** who want insights without giving up their data to a cloud service
    33→
    34→## Privacy Model
    35→
    36→**Fully local. No cloud. No accounts.**
    37→
    38→Code Insights stores all session data in a SQLite database at `~/.code-insights/data.db` on your own machine. There is no central server, no sign-up, and no data sent anywhere. The dashboard runs locally at `http://localhost:7890` — served by a Hono API process on your own machine.
    39→
    40→LLM analysis uses your own API key, stored in `~/.code-insights/config.json` (mode 0o600). API calls go directly from the local server to your chosen LLM provider — not through any Code Insights infrastructure.
    41→
    42→**Telemetry:** Anonymous, aggregate usage signals via PostHog. Opt-out model (enabled by default). Respects `CODE_INSIGHTS_TELEMETRY_DISABLED` and `DO_NOT_TRACK` environment variables. No PII collected. See `code-insights telemetry` to manage.
    43→
    44→## Core Features
    45→
    46→### Multi-Source Support
    47→
    48→| Source Tool | What's Captured |
    49→|-------------|-----------------|
    50→| **Claude Code** | JSONL sessions from `~/.claude/projects/` |
    51→| **Cursor** | Sessions from Cursor's local SQLite state |
    52→| **Codex CLI** | Rollout files from `~/.codex/sessions/` |
    53→| **Copilot CLI** | Event files from `~/.copilot/session-state/` |
    54→| **VS Code Copilot Chat** | Sessions from VS Code Copilot Chat local storage |
    55→
    56→### Insight Categories
    57→
    58→| Category | What It Captures |
    59→|----------|-----------------|
    60→| **Summary** | High-level narrative of what was accomplished |
    61→| **Decision** | Architecture choices, trade-offs, reasoning, alternatives considered |
    62→| **Learning** | Technical discoveries, mistakes, transferable knowledge |
    63→| **Technique** | Problem-solving approaches and debugging strategies |
    64→| **Prompt Quality** | Efficiency scores, wasted turns, anti-patterns, improvement suggestions |
    65→
    66→### Export
    67→
    68→Two-tier export system for turning session knowledge into shareable and actionable artifacts:
    69→
    70→**Session-level export** — per-session export of insights with two templates:
    71→- **Knowledge Base** — Human-readable markdown with full insight content
    72→- **Agent Rules** — Imperative instructions formatted for CLAUDE.md/.cursorrules
    73→
    74→**Export Page** — LLM-powered cross-session synthesis:
    75→- Reads across multiple sessions' insights to deduplicate, merge, and synthesize
    76→- Generates agent rules via LLM (not just template formatting)
    77→- 4 output formats: Agent Rules, Knowledge Brief, Obsidian (YAML frontmatter), Notion
    78→- 3 depth presets: Essential (~25 insights), Standard (~80), Comprehensive (~200)
    79→- SSE streaming with progress phases, AbortSignal support, token budget guard
    80→
    81→### Reflect & Patterns
    82→
    83→Cross-session pattern detection and synthesis, powered by session facets:
    84→
    85→**Session Facets** — Structured metadata extracted during LLM analysis for each session:
    86→- Outcome satisfaction (high/medium/low/mixed)
    87→- Workflow pattern (iterative, plan-then-execute, exploratory, debugging, etc.)
    88→- Friction points (categorized: unclear-requirements, wrong-approach, tool-limitations, etc.)
    89→- Effective patterns (what worked well and why)
    90→- Course correction tracking (whether the session changed direction and why)
    91→
    92→**CLI Commands:**
    93→- `code-insights reflect` — Generate cross-session synthesis with LLM (friction analysis, rules/skills, working style)
    94→- `code-insights reflect backfill` — Backfill facets for sessions analyzed before facet support
    95→- `code-insights stats patterns` — View pattern summary in the terminal
    96→
    97→**Dashboard Patterns Page** — Three synthesis sections:
    98→- **Friction & Wins** — Top friction categories ranked by frequency, effective patterns that worked
    99→- **Rules & Skills** — Auto-generated agent rules, skill recommendations, and hook suggestions
   100→- **Working Style** — Workflow distribution, outcome trends, session character analysis
   101→
   102→**Technical details:**
   103→- Dedicated `session_facets` SQLite table (Schema V3) with indexed scalar columns and JSON arrays
   104→- Facet extraction integrated into the existing analysis prompt (facets first, then insights)
   105→- Lightweight facet-only backfill for previously-analyzed sessions (summary + first/last 20 messages)
   106→- Friction category normalization via Levenshtein distance matching to canonical categories
   107→- Synthesis prompts pre-aggregate data in code, then feed ranked summaries to LLM for narration
   108→- Reflect snapshots cached in `reflect_snapshots` table (Schema V4) with staleness tracking
   109→- 20-session minimum threshold for meaningful synthesis; coverage warning when < 50% analyzed
   110→
   111→### Dashboard Views
   112→
   113→- **Dashboard** — Overview with activity charts
   114→- **Sessions** — Session list with source, project, date, character filters
   115→- **Session Detail** — Full session with analyze button for LLM insights
   116→- **Insights** — Browse and search generated insights
   117→- **Analytics** — Charts showing effort distribution, cost, models, projects
   118→- **Patterns** — Cross-session pattern synthesis (Friction & Wins, Rules & Skills, Working Style)
   119→- **Export** — LLM-powered export wizard (4 formats, 3 depths)
   120→- **Journal** — Session journal/notes
   121→- **Settings** — Configuration UI
   122→
   123→### CLI Stats Commands
   124→
   125→```bash
   126→code-insights stats              # Overview (last 7 days)
   127→code-insights stats cost         # Cost breakdown by project and model
   128→code-insights stats projects     # Per-project detail cards
   129→code-insights stats today        # Today's sessions with details
   130→code-insights stats models       # Model usage distribution
   131→code-insights stats patterns     # Cross-session pattern summary
   132→```
   133→
   134→## Multi-Source Architecture
   135→
   136→Code Insights uses a **provider abstraction** to support multiple AI coding tools through a common interface:
   137→
   138→```
   139→Source tool session files -> Provider (discover + parse) -> SQLite -> Dashboard / CLI stats
   140→```
   141→
   142→Each provider implements the `SessionProvider` interface (`discover()`, `parse()`, `getProviderName()`), normalizing tool-specific formats into the shared `ParsedSession` schema.
   143→
   144→### How Each Tool Stores Sessions
   145→
   146→| Tool | Format | Location (macOS) |
   147→|------|--------|-----------------|
   148→| **Claude Code** | JSONL (append-only, one JSON object per line) | `~/.claude/projects/<path>/<id>.jsonl` |
   149→| **Cursor** | SQLite key-value (`state.vscdb`, JSON blobs) | `~/Library/Application Support/Cursor/User/` |
   150→| **Codex CLI** | JSONL (event-based stream) | `~/.codex/sessions/YYYY/MM/DD/rollout-*.jsonl` |
   151→| **Copilot CLI** | JSONL (events) | `~/.copilot/session-state/{id}/events.jsonl` |
   152→| **VS Code Copilot Chat** | JSON | Platform-specific Copilot Chat storage |
   153→
   154→### Platform Paths
   155→
   156→| Tool | macOS | Linux | Windows |
   157→|------|-------|-------|---------|
   158→| Claude Code | `~/.claude/projects/` | `~/.claude/projects/` | `%USERPROFILE%\.claude\projects\` |
   159→| Cursor | `~/Library/Application Support/Cursor/User/` | `~/.config/Cursor/User/` | `%APPDATA%\Cursor\User\` |
   160→| Codex CLI | `~/.codex/sessions/` | `~/.codex/sessions/` | `%USERPROFILE%\.codex\sessions\` |
   161→| Copilot CLI | `~/.copilot/session-state/` | `~/.copilot/session-state/` | `%USERPROFILE%\.copilot\session-state\` |
   162→
   163→Adding a new source tool requires implementing the `SessionProvider` interface in `cli/src/providers/`, registering it in the provider registry, and adding dashboard display support (colors, avatars, filter options).
   164→
   165→## Tech Stack
   166→
   167→- **CLI**: Node.js (ES2022, ES Modules), Commander.js
   168→- **Database**: SQLite (`better-sqlite3`) at `~/.code-insights/data.db` — WAL mode, local, Schema V5
   169→- **Server**: Hono — lightweight API server, serves dashboard SPA at `localhost:7890`
   170→- **Dashboard**: Vite + React 19 SPA, Tailwind CSS 4 + shadcn/ui
   171→- **AI**: Multi-provider — OpenAI, Anthropic, Gemini, Ollama (your own API keys, proxied server-side)
   172→- **Telemetry**: PostHog (opt-out, anonymous device ID, no PII)
   173→- **Package manager**: pnpm (workspace monorepo: `cli/`, `dashboard/`, `server/`)
   174→
   175→## Success Metrics
   176→
   177→- Time to first insight: < 5 minutes from install
   178→- User can answer "what did I work on this week?" in one click
   179→- Decisions are searchable and linkable
   180→- Zero cloud dependencies after install
   181→

1→# Code Insights Roadmap
     2→
     3→## Overview
     4→
     5→This roadmap outlines the development phases for Code Insights. Timelines are flexible—progress is driven by priorities and availability.
     6→
     7→---
     8→
     9→## Phases 1–6 (Complete)
    10→
    11→| Phase | Goal | Key Deliverables |
    12→|-------|------|-----------------|
    13→| **1. Foundation** ✅ | End-to-end flow | CLI sync, SQLite schema, Claude Code parser, basic dashboard |
    14→| **2. Integration** ✅ | Workflow integration | Claude Code hook (`install-hook`), CLI stats suite (5 subcommands) |
    15→| **3. Intelligence** ✅ | LLM-powered insights | Multi-provider LLM (OpenAI, Anthropic, Gemini, Ollama), session analysis, 4 insight types |
    16→| **4. Feature Parity** ✅ | Local SPA + multi-source | Vite + React SPA, 5 providers (claude-code, cursor, codex-cli, copilot-cli, copilot), session-level export |
    17→| **5. Telemetry** ✅ | Anonymous usage signals | PostHog (opt-out model, 14 event types, anonymous device ID) |
    18→| **6. Distribution** ✅ | npm publish + docs | `@code-insights/cli` on npm, landing page at code-insights.app |
    19→
    20→### Pending from earlier phases
    21→- **2.3 Enhanced Filtering** — Full-text search, saved filters/bookmarks
    22→- **3.4 Learning Journal** — Auto-generated "lessons learned" from sessions
    23→- **5.2 Slash Commands** — `/insights`, `/insights today`, `/insights decisions`
    24→- **6.3 Plugin Architecture** — Custom insight extractors, dashboard widget API (deferred)
    25→
    26→---
    27→
    28→## Phase 7: Export & Knowledge Pipeline ✅
    29→
    30→**Goal:** Turn session insights into actionable knowledge artifacts via LLM-powered synthesis
    31→
    32→### Milestones
    33→
    34→- [x] **7.1 Session-Level Export Templates** (v3.5.1) ✅
    35→  - Knowledge Base template (human-readable markdown with full insight content)

    39→
    40→- [x] **7.2 LLM-Powered Export Page** (v3.6.0) ✅
    41→  - Cross-session insight synthesis via LLM (not just template formatting)
    42→  - Deduplicates overlapping learnings, resolves conflicting decisions
    43→  - Uses existing multi-provider LLM abstraction
    44→  - 3 depth presets (Essential/Standard/Comprehensive)
    45→  - SSE streaming with progress phases, AbortSignal support, token budget guard
    46→
    47→- [x] **7.3 Multi-Format Export** (v3.6.0) ✅
    48→  - Agent Rules (CLAUDE.md / .cursorrules / codex config)
    49→  - Knowledge Brief (general purpose markdown, shareable)
    50→  - Obsidian (markdown + YAML frontmatter, tags, wikilinks)
    51→  - Notion (Notion-compatible markdown with toggle blocks, callouts, tables)
    52→
    53→### Deliverables
    54→- ✅ Session-level export with two templates
    55→- ✅ LLM-powered cross-session synthesis
    56→- ✅ Multi-format output (Agent Rules, Knowledge Brief, Obsidian, Notion)
    57→
    58→---
    59→
    60→## Phase 8: Reflect & Patterns ✅
    61→
    62→**Goal:** Cross-session pattern detection and synthesis — turning individual session facets into actionable insights about friction, effective patterns, and working style
    63→
    64→### Milestones
    65→
    66→- [x] **8.1 Session Facets Infrastructure** (v3.6.1) ✅
    67→  - New `session_facets` SQLite table (Schema V3 migration)
    68→  - Per-session structured metadata: outcome, workflow, friction, effective patterns, course correction
    69→  - Facet extraction integrated into existing analysis prompt
    70→
    71→- [x] **8.2 Friction Normalization** (v3.6.1) ✅
    72→  - Canonical friction categories defined in analysis prompt
    73→  - Levenshtein distance matching (exact → distance ≤ 2 → substring → passthrough)
    74→
    75→- [x] **8.3 Server APIs** (v3.6.1) ✅
    76→  - `GET /api/facets`, `GET /api/facets/aggregated`, `POST /api/facets/backfill`
    77→  - `POST /api/reflect/generate` — SSE streaming LLM synthesis
    78→
    79→- [x] **8.4 CLI Commands** (v3.6.1) ✅
    80→  - `code-insights reflect` — Cross-session synthesis with LLM
    81→  - `code-insights stats patterns` — Pattern summary in terminal
    82→
    83→- [x] **8.5 Dashboard Patterns Page** (v3.6.1) ✅
    84→  - Three-tab layout: Friction & Wins, Rules & Skills, Working Style
    85→  - ARIA-accessible tab navigation, copy-to-clipboard
    86→
    87→- [x] **8.6 Persistence & Guardrails** (v3.6.1) ✅
    88→  - Schema V4: `reflect_snapshots` table with upsert semantics
    89→  - 20-session minimum threshold, < 50% coverage warning
    90→  - Snapshot auto-load, staleness indicator, project filter
    91→
    92→- [x] **8.7 Facet Backfill CLI** (v3.6.1) ✅

    95→
    96→- [x] **8.8 Friction Category Refinement** (v3.6.1) ✅
    97→  - Improved canonical categories, reduced misclassification
    98→
    99→- [x] **8.9 Patterns Page Refinement** (v3.6.1) ✅
   100→  - Outcome badge and usage stats on session detail

   102→
   103→### Deliverables
   104→- ✅ Session facets with Schema V3 migration
   105→- ✅ Cross-session pattern synthesis via LLM
   106→- ✅ CLI reflect command and stats patterns subcommand
   107→- ✅ Dashboard Patterns page with three synthesis sections

   109→
   110→---
   111→
   112→## Version Milestones
   113→
   114→| Version | Phase | Key Features | Status |
   115→|---------|-------|--------------|--------|
   116→| 0.1.0 | 1 | CLI sync, SQLite schema, basic dashboard | ✅ Done |
   117→| 0.2.0 | 1 | Smart titles, session classification | ✅ Done |
   118→| 0.3.0 | 2 | Claude Code hook, CLI stats commands | ✅ Done |
   119→| 0.4.0 | 3 | Multi-LLM analysis, bulk analyze | ✅ Done |
   120→| 0.5.0 | 4 | Vite SPA + Hono server, embedded dashboard | ✅ Done |
   121→| 0.6.0 | 4 | Multi-source support (Cursor, Codex, Copilot CLI, VS Code Copilot Chat) | ✅ Done |
   122→| 3.0.0 | 6 | npm publish, local-first migration, README rewrite | ✅ Done |
   123→| 3.1.0 | 6 | Server runtime deps fix, dashboard path fallback | ✅ Done |
   124→| 3.2.0 | 4 | Dashboard polish — skeletons, ErrorCard, toasts, bundle audit | ✅ Done |
   125→| 3.3.0 | 5 | PostHog anonymous telemetry (opt-out model) | ✅ Done |
   126→| 3.4.0 | — | Multi-source parser fixes (Codex, Cursor, Copilot), agent message rendering | ✅ Done |
   127→| 3.5.1 | 7 | Session-level export templates (Knowledge Base, Agent Rules), prompt quality | ✅ Done |
   128→| 3.6.0 | 7 | LLM-powered Export Page (cross-session synthesis, 4 formats, SSE streaming) | ✅ Done |
   129→| 3.6.1 | 8 | Reflect & Patterns (facets, friction normalization, synthesis, Patterns page) | ✅ Done |
   130→
   131→---
   132→
   133→## What's Next
   134→
   135→- Test suite expansion (Vitest)
   136→- Slash commands for quick insights from the terminal

   141→---
   142→
   143→## Contributing
   144→
